Article.ArticleTitle: Angiotensin-Neprilysin Inhibition and Left Ventricular Assist Device Therapy: Primary Results of the ENVAD-HF Trial.

Abstract.AbstractText: The role of heart failure-specific therapies in left ventricular assist device (LVAD) recipients is unclear, and observational data suggest improved outcomes with neurohormonal blockers.

ENVAD-HF (Multicenter, Randomized, Open-Label, Parallel Group, Study to Evaluate the Use of Sacubitril/Valsartan in HeartMate 3 LVAD Recipients) sought to evaluate the safety and tolerability of the angiotensin-neprilysin inhibitor sacubitril/valsartan vs standard of care (SOC) for managing blood pressure (BP) in HeartMate 3 LVAD recipients.

ENVAD-HF was a prospective multicenter, randomized, open-label study of sacubitril/valsartan vs SOC for managing BP (mean arterial pressure goal: 75-90 mm Hg) in stable LVAD recipients with 12-month follow-up. The composite primary endpoint was time to death, deterioration in renal function, hyperkalemia, or symptomatic hypotension leading to drug withdrawal. Exploratory endpoints included clinical and biomarker assessments and patient-reported outcomes.

In 60 randomized patients (30 in each arm), sacubitril/valsartan compared with SOC demonstrated an HR of 0.42 (95% CI: 0.08-2.18; P = 0.30) for the primary endpoint at 12 months. Two primary endpoints were reached in the sacubitril/valsartan group (1 death and 1 symptomatic hypotension event) compared with 5 in the SOC group (2 deaths, 2 worsening renal function events, and 1 symptomatic hypotension event). Numerical trends in favor of sacubitril/valsartan were noted for other exploratory endpoints, including a reduced number of BP medications (difference: -1.09 [95% CI: -1.52 to -0.66]; P < 0.0001) and a significantly better Kansas City Cardiomyopathy Questionnaire-Overall Summary Score (improvement: +10.6 [95% CI: 2.6-18.7]; P = 0.011).

ENVAD-HF, a prospective randomized controlled trial of angiotensin-neprilysin inhibition in stable HeartMate 3 LVAD recipients, demonstrated the safety and tolerability of this therapy in this unique population. The trial forms the basis for a pivotal trial to investigate the usefulness of HF-specific therapies in the LVAD population. (Sacubitril/Valsartan in Left Ventricular Assist Device Recipients [ENVAD-HF], NCT04103554; A multicENter, randomized, open-label, parallel group, pilot study to evaluate the use of sacubitril/valsartan in HeartMate 3 LVAD recipients, 2019-003888-22).

Chemical.NameOfSubstance: sacubitril and valsartan sodium hydrate drug combination

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Funding Support and Author Disclosures ENVAD-HF was an investigator-initiated trial coordinated at the University of Zagreb School of Medicine as the sponsor institution. The trial was supported by an institutional grant from Novartis and Abbott’s Heart Failure Division. These organizations were not involved in the design, execution, analysis, presentation, or publication decisions of the study. Dr Cikes has received investigator-initiated research grants to Novartis, Abbott, and Pfizer; clinical study contracts with Novo Nordisk and CorVia; and has served in advisory roles for and received speaker honoraria and travel grants from Abbott, Amgen, Amicus Therapeutics, AstraZeneca, Bayer, Boehringer Ingelheim, Bristol Myers Squibb, GE HealthCare, Novartis, Novo Nordisk, Pfizer, Roche, Swixx, Takeda, Teva Pharmaceutical, and Viatris. Dr Planinc has received an investigator-initiated research grant to Pfizer; reports clinical study contracts with CPC Clinical Research; and has served in advisory roles for and received speaker honoraria and travel grants from AstraZeneca, Bayer, Belupo, Berlin Chemie Menarini, Boehringer Ingelheim, Cardio Medical, Evidera, Krka Pharma, Novartis, Novo Nordisk, Pfizer, PharmaS, Swixx, Teva Pharmaceutical, and Zentiva. Dr Brugts has received an independent research grant for ISS from Abbott to his institute; and has had speaker engagements with AstraZeneca, Abbott, Boehringer Ingelheim, Bayer, Novartis, and Vifor in the past 5 years. Dr Claggett has consulted for Alexion, Alnylam, Cardior, Cardurion, Corvia, CVRx, Eli Lilly, Intellia, Rocket, and Valo. Dr Jakus has received speaker honoraria and travel grants from Abbott, AstraZeneca, Bayer, Boehringer Ingelheim, Krka, Novo Nordisk, Pfizer, and Teva Pharmaceutical. Dr Loncaric has received speaker honoraria and travel grants from GE HealthCare, Pfizer, Swixx, Zentiva, Boehringer, Amgen, and Sandoz. The University Medical Centre Utrecht, which employs Dr van Laake, has received speaker fees and research grants from Medtronic, Abbott, Novartis, and Vifor. Dr Mehra has received payments made to his institution by Abbott for consulting; consulting fees from Moderna, Paragonix, FIRE-1 Foundry, and the Baim Institute for Clinical Research; and has served on advisory boards of Second Heart Assist (uncompensated), Transmedics, NuPulseCV, Leviticus, and FineHeart. Dr Solomon has received research grants from Alexion, Alnylam, Applied Therapeutics, AstraZeneca, Bellerophon, Bayer, Bristol Myers Squibb, Boston Scientific, Cytokinetics, Edgewise, Eidos/BridgeBio, Gossamer, GlaxoSmithKline, Ionis, Intellia, Lilly, National Institutes of Health/National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute, Novartis, Novo Nordisk, Respicardia, Sanofi Pasteur, Tenaya, Theracos, and Us2.ai; and has consulted for Abbott, Action, Akros, Alexion, Alnylam, Amgen, Arena, AskBio, AstraZeneca, Bayer, Bristol Myers Squibb, Cardior, Cardurion, Corvia, Cytokinetics, GlaxoSmithKline, Lilly, Novartis, Novo Nordisk, Roche, Theracos, Quantum Genomics, Tenaya, Sanofi Pasteur, DiNAQOR, Tremeau, CellProThera, Moderna, American Regent, Sarepta, Lexicon, AnaCardio, Akros, Valo, Synhale, and Recordati. All other authors have reported that they have no relationships relevant to the contents of this paper to disclose.
